hook: You already know how to find the area under a single curve and compute antiderivatives — that's a powerful toolkit. But the real world rarely asks 'what's the area under this one function?'; it asks questions like 'how much material do I need to build this shape?' or 'what was the average temperature today?' This unit hands you the keys to answer those questions, turning integration into a Swiss Army knife for geometry, physics, and beyond.
concept: Every topic here takes the core idea of integration — summing infinitely many infinitesimally small pieces — and aims it at a new geometric or physical quantity, from areas and volumes to distances and curve lengths. In formal terms, these are all applications of the definite integral as a tool for accumulation.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to compute the area of a region sandwiched between two functions by integrating their difference, $\int_a^b [f(x) - g(x)]\,dx$.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Volume by Disk and Washer Methods
topic_roadmap.description: Master the technique of slicing a solid of revolution into thin circular disks (or washers with holes) and integrating their areas to find the total volume.
Apply cylindrical shells as an alternative slicing strategy to find volumes of revolution, especially when the disk/washer method leads to awkward integrals.
You will learn to compute the average value of a continuous function over an interval using $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{b-a}\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$, connecting the formula to the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals.
Apply integration to recover position and displacement from velocity (and velocity from acceleration), bringing kinematics problems full circle from the differentiation you did earlier.
You will learn to set up and evaluate the arc length integral $\int_a^b \sqrt{1 + [f'(x)]^2}\,dx$ to measure the exact length of a smooth curve.
Master the surface area integral to calculate the exact area of the surface generated when a curve is rotated around an axis, extending your arc length skills into three dimensions.
